mappings.rationaleAI: Theory and practice of measurement covering mathematical frameworks, SI units, accuracy, and precision directly addresses theory of measurement (A), units and standards (B), principles of instrument operation (C), and common measuring instruments (D).

Light microscopy, electron microscopy, transmission microscopy, scanning tunnelling microscopy, and atomic force microscopy directly address microscopes of optical, electron, and acoustic types (E.1.a).
Optical and electromagnetic telescope design from Galileo to space-based observatories including infrared, ultraviolet, X-ray, and gamma-ray systems directly addresses telescopes (E.1.b).

Navigational instruments including magnetic compasses (D.2.c.i), astrolabes and sextants (D.2.c.ii), and chronometers directly address instruments for measuring position and navigational techniques (G.7).

History of Western astronomy covering observational and instrumental developments from Tycho Brahe's instruments to post-WWII technology addresses telescopes (E.1.b) and astronomical observations (G.6).
Experimental discoveries using particle accelerators at facilities like the Large Hadron Collider relate to particle accelerators (F.3) and particle detectors (F.7).
Mass spectrometry and synchrotron radiation techniques for measuring radioisotopes connect to instruments for measuring ionizing radiation (D.2.f) and mass spectrometers (F.4).
Seismic wave analysis, satellite-based gravity and magnetic field measurement, and GPS technology connect to instruments for measuring gravity (D.2.d), seismographs (F.6), and surveying (G.1).
Atmospheric measurement of temperature, pressure, and humidity connects to barometers and manometers (D.2.a), thermometers (D.1.d), and meteorological measurement (G.5).
Physics of light covering electromagnetic radiation measurement, lasers, and optical technologies connects to photometers and light meters (D.1.f), spectroscopes (E.1.c), and interferometers (E.1.d).
X-ray, electron, and neutron diffraction techniques for determining crystal structures connect to spectroscopes and spectrographs (E.1.c) as instruments for observing atomic-level phenomena.
Isotope ratio measurement techniques and radioactive decay analysis connect to instruments for measuring ionizing radiation (D.2.f) and mass spectrometers (F.4).
Computing technology covering data processing, embedded processors, and network communication relates to instrumentation and control systems (H) through computerized measurement and control.
Photography from daguerreotype origins through digital-era imaging connects to cameras as instruments for recording phenomena (E.2).

History of timekeeping from ancient civilizations to modernity covering calendar systems and standardization addresses standards and techniques for measurement of time (B.2) and devices for measuring time (D.1.c).

Statistical methodology covering probability, estimation, and experimental design provides mathematical foundations for measurement theory (A) and the principles by which measuring instruments operate (C).
Ocean science covering physical processes, seafloor topography, and tectonic processes relates to oceanographic measurement (G.4).
Mathematical modeling and data analysis methods used across sciences provide the mathematical frameworks underlying measurement theory (A).

Covers time dilation and GPS technology applications of general relativity, relevant to standards and techniques for measurement of time and the theoretical foundations underlying precision timekeeping.

Covers probability theory including the mathematical framework for modeling imprecise measurements, central limit theorem, and standard deviation, directly relevant to the theory of measurement and its statistical foundations.
